title: 🟡 Intervals — Dry Run & Quiz
oneSentence: Trace Merge Intervals, Insert Interval, and Meeting Rooms II.

mergeIntervals.title: Merge Intervals
mergeIntervals.description: Given an array of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals.
mergeIntervals.story: Sort by start times, then merge if current end >= next start.

steps.desc: Current interval: [1,3].
Compare [1,3] and [1,3]. Overlap. End is max(3, 3) = 3.
Compare [1,3] and [2,6]. Overlap (3 >= 2). End is max(3, 6) = 6. Current is [1,6].
Compare [1,6] and [8,10]. No overlap (6 < 8). Current becomes [8,10]. Add to res.
Compare [8,10] and [15,18]. No overlap (10 < 15). Current becomes [15,18]. Add to res.

quiz.q: Why do we modify `current[1]` in place?
options: Because `current` is added to the list by reference, modifying it updates the interval inside the result list.
It saves memory, though it doesn't affect the list.
quiz.explain: Since `current` is an array reference stored in the list, modifying `current[1]` directly updates the element in the result list without needing to re-add it.

insertInterval.title: Insert Interval
insertInterval.description: Insert a new interval into a sorted array of non-overlapping intervals and merge if necessary.
insertInterval.story: Add strictly before, merge overlapping, add strictly after.
code: public static int[][] solve(int[][] intervals, int[] newInterval) {

steps.desc: Interval [1,3], newInterval [2,5]. 3 >= 2, so it doesn't end before newInterval. Skip first while loop.
Overlap check: [1,3] starts at 1 <= 5. Merge: min(2,1)=1, max(5,3)=5. newInterval becomes [1,5]. i=1.
Next interval [6,9]. Starts at 6 > 5. No overlap. Exit second while loop.
Add merged newInterval [1,5] to res.
Add remaining intervals: [6,9].

quiz.q: Why don't we need to sort the intervals first?
options: The problem statement guarantees they are already sorted by start time.
Sorting is handled by the while loops.
We only care about the new interval, so sorting is unnecessary.
quiz.explain: The problem states the input intervals are already sorted and non-overlapping, saving us the O(N log N) sorting step.

meetingRoomsII.title: Meeting Rooms II
meetingRoomsII.description: Given an array of meeting time intervals, find the minimum number of conference rooms required.
meetingRoomsII.story: Separate starts and ends. Two pointers. If start < end, need a room. Else, room freed.

steps.desc: Extract and sort starts: [0, 5, 15].
Extract and sort ends: [10, 20, 30].
s=0 (0), e=0 (10). starts[0] < ends[0]. rooms=1, maxRooms=1. s=1.
s=1 (5), e=0 (10). starts[1] < ends[0]. rooms=2, maxRooms=2. s=2.
s=2 (15), e=0 (10). starts[2] >= ends[0]. Room freed. rooms=1. e=1.
s=2 (15), e=1 (20). starts[2] < ends[1]. rooms=2, maxRooms=2. s=3.
s=3. Loop terminates. Return maxRooms=2.

quiz.q: Why do we sort start and end times separately?
options: We only care about how many meetings overlap at any point, independent of which specific meeting ends.
To match each start time with its corresponding end time.
To reduce time complexity to O(N).
quiz.explain: By sorting them separately, we treat starts as 'add a room' and ends as 'free a room' events, effectively tracking the active overlap count across all rooms.
